transcription.status: NUMBERS VERIFIED (blind dual entry, all 60 rows + checksums clean); JUDGMENTS PENDING Hassan's ratification — verified stays false until then
transcription.verification_method: blind dual machine transcription + human ratification of judgments
transcription.verification_note: Blind dual entry (Session 13, D13.0): the PDF was rendered to page images and Tables 2-3 transcribed TWICE independently by two agents that never saw this JSON, then diffed against each other and against this file. RESULT — numeric data is exact: all 26 Policy-Space rows (Table 2, pp.125-126) and all 34 Competitive-Space rows (Table 3, pp.130-131) match on position, capability, and salience; the two blind reads agree on every cell; the printed Exercised Power = Influence x Salience / 100 column reproduces on every row (used as a checksum); the JSON's capability = the paper's Influence column; the derived Exercised Power column is correctly omitted. Continua are faithful to the paper: case 1 to Figure 1 (p.129); case 2 to Figure 6 (p.138), whose printed markers are 0=status quo, 10=end of patronage, 30=transparent tendering, 60='Divest major identified assets; e.g. pipelines' (open access), 100=break up CNPC — i.e. the Position-60 marker the long-run coding leans on IS printed in the paper. Both published_model_forecast notes match the paper's results (Policy converges ~30, sweep 15-45; Competitive Position 20-40, sweep 30-50). OPEN JUDGMENTS FOR HASSAN (not decided by the machine): (a) the near-term outcome codings 25/25; (b) the long-run codings 55/60, incl. whether the Dec-2019 PipeChina divestiture = the paper's printed Position-60 marker; (c) the horizon rule — NOTE the paper states NO calendar horizon (its 10 'turns' are bargaining rounds, not years; the only temporal phrase is 'in the near term'), so the by_2017 / by_2020 / 5-year framing below is an imposed convention, not the paper's own. Quote Hassan's ratification here and flip verified to true only after he answers (a)-(c).

cases.title: Private-sector space in China's upstream oil & gas (policy dimension)
cases.source: Efird, Lester & Wise (2016), 'Analyzing Coalitions in China's Policy Formulation', Journal of East Asian Studies 16, 117-145, doi:10.1017/jea.2015.4, Table 2

continuum.label: Openness of upstream oil & gas to private participation
continuum.anchor_100: Policy environment open and conducive to private participation

markers.15: private allowed only in JV with an SOE (approx. status quo 2014)
markers.30: ad hoc participation by government approval

published_model_forecast.value_note: convergence around the Xi-led cluster at ~30; little prospect of opening (paper text)

near_term_by_2017.basis: PROPOSED by Claude — verify: little near-term opening; participation remained JV/approval-based; matches paper's own near-term claim

long_run_by_2020.basis: PROPOSED by Claude — verify: May 2020 Ministry of Natural Resources opened oil & gas exploration/production licensing to qualified domestic private and foreign firms — a structural opening, though practical penetration stayed limited

continuum.label: Degree of reform reducing CNPC's dominance

markers.60: required divestiture of major assets, e.g. pipelines run on open access

published_model_forecast.value_note: reform reflecting a Position between 20 and 40; breakup implausible; scenario sweep bounds outcome ~30-50 even under extreme leader positions (paper text)

near_term_by_2017.basis: PROPOSED by Claude — verify: anti-corruption purges of CNPC leadership, tendering/mixed-ownership pilots, no structural divestiture

long_run_by_2020.basis: PROPOSED by Claude — verify: Dec 2019 creation of PipeChina; CNPC (with Sinopec/CNOOC) pipeline assets divested into a national open-access pipeline company, operational Oct 2020 — matching this continuum's explicit Position-60 marker; CNPC core not broken up

notes: DOMAIN CAVEAT: these are domestic-elite bargaining cases (cooperative regime), NOT coercive interstate crises — they extend out-of-domain validation beyond the EU but do NOT settle the coercive verdict. The coercive classics (Red Flag over Hong Kong, Iran 1984, Feder cases) remain the quest.
HORIZON RULE (proposed): each case carries two outcome readings — the paper's own stated horizon (primary, used for the gate) and a 5-year reading (secondary, reported not gated).
BONUS COLUMN: the published KTAB forecast enables three-way scoring on these cases: Schelling challenge vs compromise mean vs the incumbent KTAB SMP.
